title: Bending Spoons ($BSP): IPO Due Diligence Memo
question: Is Bending Spoons a differentiated permanent owner/operator of digital products, or a PE-like software roll-up whose growth depends on M&A, debt, restructuring and pricing power?
finding: Real business, real complexity - and a conglomerate, not a roll-up. Bending Spoons holds 16+ unrelated digital businesses (AOL, Eventbrite, Vimeo, Evernote, WeTransfer and more) across six unrelated end-markets, with one genuine roll-up pocket in video/creator tools. Revenue grew from $387.1m (2023) to $1.306bn (2025), but the cash flow tells a sharper story than the 51% adjusted margin: growth is bought not earned (organic only 7-13%), the businesses fund only ~5% of the acquisition spend (debt and equity fund the rest), interest now eats most of the operating profit (cover ~1.9x in 2025 and ~1.3x in Q1 2026 as debt hit $4.48bn), and the 2.19x leverage the company shows becomes ~6x on trailing adjusted operating income and ~13x on GAAP operating income. So the IPO reads as equity to refuel and de-lever a capital-hungry acquisition machine - a yellow flag on the model's appetite for capital - but not a distress raise: no going-concern flag, no near-term maturity wall (57% of the debt matures after 2030), ongoing debt-market access, and a partial founder sell-down inside the offering. Price it as a leveraged capital-allocation conglomerate (Teledyne/Danaher archetype), not a focused, organically compounding software business.
